questions.stem: In an ML interview, how do you crisply distinguish supervised learning from unsupervised learning?
options: Supervised trains on labeled targets; unsupervised finds structure without labels
Supervised never needs data; unsupervised always needs labels for every row
They differ only by programming language used, not by the learning setup
Unsupervised always predicts a continuous y; supervised only clusters points
questions.explanation: Labels define the prediction target in supervised learning; unsupervised seeks patterns without y.

questions.stem: Screening question: why split data into train, validation, and test sets instead of one pile?
options: All three splits must be identical copies of the same rows for fairness
Train fits; validation tunes; test estimates final generalization once
Validation replaces test forever so you never need a held-out set
Test is only for hyperparameter search; train is only for reporting
questions.explanation: Separation prevents leaking selection decisions into the final performance estimate.

questions.stem: Bias–variance tradeoff: what does high variance typically look like on train versus validation?
options: High train and validation error that stay almost equally bad
Perfect validation accuracy with random guessing on the training set
Very low train error but much higher validation error (overfitting pattern)
Identical errors on every split with no sensitivity to model capacity
questions.explanation: High-variance models memorize train quirks and fail to generalize to validation.

questions.stem: Precision versus recall: which one-line definitions should you give in an interview?
options: Precision equals accuracy; recall equals the learning rate chosen
Recall is only for regression; precision is only for clustering tasks
They are interchangeable metrics that always move in the same direction
Precision: of predicted positives, how many true; recall: of actual positives, how many found
questions.explanation: Precision guards false positives; recall guards missed true positives.

questions.stem: When would you prefer F1 over raw accuracy in a classification interview scenario?
options: Imbalanced classes where accuracy can look high while missing the minority class
When every class has exactly equal prior and zero cost asymmetry
Only for unsupervised clustering where labels do not exist at all
When the model outputs continuous scores without any decision threshold
questions.explanation: F1 balances precision and recall and is more informative under class imbalance.

questions.stem: Overfitting — what practical signs and responses should you mention in an interview setting?
options: Val error always lower than train; increase capacity until train fails
Train ≪ val error; simplify, regularize, get more data, or early-stop
Overfitting means the optimizer failed to decrease the training loss
It is fixed only by removing the validation set from the pipeline
questions.explanation: Gap between train and val signals memorization; capacity control and data help.

questions.stem: Underfitting versus overfitting: how do you tell them apart from learning curves?
options: Underfit: zero train error only; overfit: zero validation error only
They produce identical curves; only wall-clock time distinguishes them
Underfit: high train and val error; overfit: low train, high val error
Underfit appears only with neural nets; overfit only with linear models
questions.explanation: Both errors high ⇒ underfit; train good but val poor ⇒ overfit.

questions.stem: Feature scaling: when is it especially important and why do interviewers ask about it?
options: Tree ensembles always require z-score scaling before every split decision
Scaling deletes outliers automatically so you never need robust methods
It only matters for visualization dashboards, never for model training
Distance-based and gradient methods suffer if features have wildly different scales
questions.explanation: Scale mismatch distorts distances and slows or skews many optimizers.

questions.stem: L1 versus L2 regularization — what difference is most interview-relevant to explain?
options: L1 can drive coefficients to zero (sparsity); L2 shrinks but rarely zeros
L2 deletes features; L1 only smooths the loss landscape for SGD
They are identical penalties with different Greek letters in papers
L1 applies only to neural nets; L2 applies only to decision trees
questions.explanation: L1 encourages sparse solutions; L2 prefers small dense weights.

questions.stem: Cross-validation: why use k-fold instead of a single train/val split when data is limited?
options: It guarantees zero variance in every hyperparameter search result
Averages performance across folds so estimates are less split-dependent
It removes the need for a final held-out test set in all cases
Folding duplicates rows until the dataset looks infinitely large
questions.explanation: Multiple folds reuse data for more stable validation estimates.

questions.stem: Classification vs regression: how do you frame the target type clearly in an interview?
options: Regression always outputs probabilities; classification never does
They use identical loss functions and evaluation metrics always
Classification predicts discrete labels; regression predicts continuous values
Classification is unsupervised; regression is the only supervised task
questions.explanation: Target type drives loss choice, metrics, and model family.

questions.stem: Train/validation leakage: which practice should you call out as dangerous in interviews?
options: Holding out a test set untouched until the final evaluation step
Tuning hyperparameters only on the validation fold, not the test fold
Shuffling before a random split when rows are i.i.d. observations
Fitting scalers or selecting features using the full dataset before splitting
questions.explanation: Preprocessing fit on all data leaks future information into training.

questions.stem: Ensemble methods at a high level: what core idea should you state cleanly and accurately?
options: Combine multiple models so errors cancel and predictive power improves
Train one giant model that is forced to memorize every training example
Replace labeled data with random labels to increase diversity
Always average predictions with equal weights and never learn weights
questions.explanation: Diversity plus aggregation is the ensemble principle behind bagging/boosting.

questions.stem: Bagging versus boosting — which contrast is most accurate for ML interview answers?
options: Boosting always reduces variance only; bagging only reduces bias always
Bagging trains in parallel on bootstrap samples; boosting trains sequentially on residuals
They are synonyms for the same algorithm invented under two names
Bagging requires neural nets; boosting works only with linear regression
questions.explanation: Bagging averages parallel learners; boosting focuses later models on hard cases.

questions.stem: Confusion matrix: if false positives are very costly, which metric do you emphasize first?
options: Recall only, because false positives never affect precision by definition
R-squared, because it directly counts false positives in classification
Precision (and related costs), because FP hurt precision among predicted positives
Silhouette score, the standard cost-sensitive classification metric
questions.explanation: High precision means few false alarms among predicted positives.

questions.stem: Why is a held-out test set evaluated only once (or rarely) in careful ML practice?
options: Test metrics are illegal to compute more than once in any ML library
The test set shrinks each time you evaluate until no rows remain
Optimizers require the test loss gradient on every training step
Repeated peeking turns the test set into another validation set and biases estimates
questions.explanation: Test is a final, rarely touched estimate of generalization.

questions.stem: Feature engineering vs automatic deep features: what pragmatic view should you give?
options: Good features still matter; deep models learn representations but domain features help
Feature engineering is obsolete for every problem after 2012 without exception
Neural nets cannot use engineered features by architectural law
Only unsupervised methods benefit from human-designed features
questions.explanation: Representation learning helps, but domain signal and clean features remain valuable.

questions.stem: Standardization (z-score) versus min-max scaling: when might min-max be preferred?
options: When outliers dominate and you want them to stretch the entire scale heavily
When you need features bounded to a fixed range for certain models or activations
When tree-based models require all features in [0,1] to split correctly
When labels are missing and you must invent targets from feature ranges
questions.explanation: Min-max maps to a range; beware outlier sensitivity compared with robust scaling.

questions.stem: What does a high-bias model imply about capacity relative to the problem complexity?
options: Capacity is infinite; it memorizes noise and fails only on training data
The learning rate is zero so gradients never update any parameters
Capacity is too low / assumptions too strong; it underfits systematic patterns
The validation set was empty so bias cannot be estimated at all
questions.explanation: Bias reflects restrictive assumptions that miss the true relationship.

questions.stem: Imbalanced classification: besides F1, what practical techniques should you mention?
options: Delete the minority class so accuracy becomes trivially perfect
Always switch to unsupervised clustering and ignore labels completely
Report only training accuracy after oversampling the majority class
Resampling, class weights, threshold tuning, and appropriate ranking metrics
questions.explanation: Weighting, sampling, thresholds, and PR/ROC-style metrics address imbalance.

questions.stem: Interview deep-dive: how does regularization reduce overfitting without collecting more data?
options: It penalizes complexity (weights/capacity) so the model prefers simpler fits
It duplicates the validation set into training until losses match exactly
It forces train error to zero while ignoring validation performance
It replaces the loss with accuracy so gradients become unnecessary
questions.explanation: Complexity penalties shrink effective capacity and improve generalization.

questions.stem: Early stopping as regularization: what signal do you monitor and why stop training?
options: Stop when training loss is still decreasing rapidly every epoch
Stop when validation loss stops improving to avoid fitting noise later
Stop only after test accuracy peaks, then continue training on test
Stop randomly each epoch to inject stochasticity into the parameters
questions.explanation: Validation plateau or worsening indicates further training harms generalization.

questions.stem: Bias–variance: if both train and val error are high, what is your first diagnosis?
options: Classic high variance — immediately prune capacity and add heavy L1
Perfect fit — ship the model because errors being equal means success
Likely underfitting — try a richer model, better features, or longer training
Label leakage — both errors high always means the target leaked
questions.explanation: High/high errors point to underfit before you chase variance fixes.

questions.stem: Precision–recall tradeoff: raising the positive threshold typically does what to metrics?
options: Always raises both precision and recall simultaneously without cost
Converts the classifier into a clustering algorithm automatically
Leaves PR unchanged because thresholds never affect hard decisions
Often raises precision and lowers recall for the positive class
questions.explanation: Stricter thresholds yield fewer positives (higher precision, lower recall).

questions.stem: Macro vs micro F1: when does an interviewer expect you to prefer the macro average?
options: When each class should count equally regardless of support size
When you only care about overall decision count weighted by frequency
When regression targets must be averaged across folds
When unsupervised silhouette scores need an F1-style summary
questions.explanation: Macro averages per-class F1; micro aggregates contributions by instance.

questions.stem: Why can accuracy be misleading even at 99% on a rare-event detection task?
options: Accuracy formulas forbid values above 50% for rare-event problems
Predicting the majority class always can hit ~99% while missing all events
99% accuracy always implies perfect recall on the rare positive class
Rare-event tasks cannot use accuracy because labels are continuous
questions.explanation: Majority-class baselines inflate accuracy when positives are rare.

questions.stem: Ridge vs Lasso naming: which statement matches common interview vocabulary exactly?
options: Ridge is L1; Lasso is L2; Elastic Net forbids both penalties
Ridge and Lasso are tree pruning heuristics unrelated to linear models
Ridge is L2; Lasso is L1; Elastic Net mixes both penalties
Lasso only applies to neural dropout; Ridge only to batch norm
questions.explanation: Standard names: Ridge equals L2, Lasso equals L1, Elastic Net combines both.

questions.stem: Random forests: what two ingredients make the bagged trees more diverse and useful?
options: Training every tree on the identical full dataset with the same features
Sharing a single tree structure and only randomizing leaf labels
Replacing impurity criteria with unsupervised clustering at every node
Bootstrap samples of rows plus random feature subsets at splits
questions.explanation: Row bagging and feature randomness decorrelate trees for better averaging.

questions.stem: Gradient boosting intuition: what does each new tree typically try to accomplish?
options: Fit the residual errors (or gradients) left by the current ensemble
Ignore previous trees and restart from random labels each round
Maximize train accuracy by memorizing one random feature only
Delete half the previous trees to keep the ensemble size constant
questions.explanation: Boosting stages correct remaining mistakes via residual or gradient fitting.

questions.stem: Time-series split caution: why is random k-fold often wrong for temporal data?
options: k-fold cannot run when timestamps are present in any dataframe
It leaks future information into training by shuffling across time
Temporal data forbids validation sets of any kind by definition
Random folds always produce lower variance than chronological splits
questions.explanation: Respect time order; use walk-forward or time-based validation.

questions.stem: Calibration of probabilities: what problem does it address beyond ranking quality?
options: It forces accuracy and F1 to become mathematically identical
It removes the need for any decision threshold in production
Predicted probabilities should match true outcome frequencies
It converts unsupervised embeddings into supervised labels
questions.explanation: Calibration aligns score magnitudes with empirical probabilities.

questions.stem: Multicollinearity: how does it affect linear models in a solid interview answer?
options: It always improves generalization by duplicating useful signal
Tree models crash whenever any two features correlate above 0.1
It only appears in unsupervised PCA and never in regression
Correlated features make coefficients unstable and hard to interpret
questions.explanation: Collinearity inflates coefficient variance; regularization or PCA can help.

questions.stem: Train/val/test ratios: what principle matters more than a fixed 70/15/15 rule?
options: Enough train to learn; enough val to tune; enough test to estimate reliably
Always use 99% train and 0.5% each for val and test regardless of N
Validation must be larger than train to avoid overfitting completely
Test must equal train size so metrics are comparable by construction
questions.explanation: Sizes depend on data volume and metric variance, not a magic ratio.

questions.stem: ROC-AUC vs PR-AUC: when is PR-AUC often more informative for practitioners?
options: When negatives do not exist and only regression metrics apply
Severe class imbalance where positives are rare and PR focuses on them
When you need a threshold-free metric that ignores ranking quality
Only when the model is unsupervised and labels are unavailable
questions.explanation: PR curves emphasize positive-class performance under imbalance.

questions.stem: Data leakage via target encoding: what careful practice should you describe clearly?
options: Encode using the full dataset including test labels for maximum signal
Replace all categoricals with the global target mean after seeing test
Compute encodings within folds / using only training rows to avoid peeking
Target encoding is safe always because categories are not numeric
questions.explanation: Target statistics must not use rows that should remain unseen.

questions.stem: Curse of dimensionality at a practical level: what happens as feature count explodes?
options: Every algorithm becomes linear-time and overfitting disappears
Validation error always drops monotonically with more random features
Feature scaling becomes unnecessary because all norms coincide
Distances concentrate; models need more data; sparsity and noise grow
questions.explanation: High dimensions make neighborhood methods and sample needs harder.

questions.stem: Holdout vs bootstrap resampling for error estimates — key interview contrast to state?
options: Holdout uses a fixed split; bootstrap resamples with replacement many times
Bootstrap forbids replacement; holdout requires infinite validation sets
They produce identical estimates by mathematical identity always
Holdout is only for unsupervised; bootstrap only for reinforcement learning
questions.explanation: Different resampling schemes trade bias and variance of the estimate itself.

questions.stem: Why might you choose a simpler linear model over a complex ensemble in production?
options: Linear models always beat ensembles on every benchmark without exception
Interpretability, latency, stability, and adequate accuracy for the business
Ensembles cannot be deployed because they lack serialization formats
Regulators forbid any model that uses more than one decision tree
questions.explanation: Operational and explainability constraints often favor simpler models.

questions.stem: Stratified sampling in classification splits: what problem does stratification solve?
options: Guarantees that every fold contains only one class for purity
Removes the need for metrics other than accuracy forever
Preserves class proportions across train/val/test when classes are imbalanced
Forces features to be Gaussian before any model training begins
questions.explanation: Stratification keeps rare classes represented in each split.

questions.stem: Online vs batch learning: what distinction should you articulate clearly in interviews?
options: Online learning forbids gradients; batch learning forbids any loss function
They differ only in GPU brand, not in data arrival assumptions
Batch learning cannot shuffle; online learning cannot use regularization
Online updates incrementally as data arrives; batch trains on a fixed dataset
questions.explanation: Streaming updates versus offline training on a static corpus.

questions.stem: Senior interview: how do you choose between collecting more data and adding regularization?
options: Diagnose bias/variance via curves; more data helps variance; capacity/features help bias
Always collect more data first because regularization never helps generalization
Always add L1 first because more data cannot reduce variance by theory
Flip a coin; both interventions have identical effects on every learning curve
questions.explanation: Learning-curve diagnosis guides whether data or model capacity is the bottleneck.

questions.stem: Nested cross-validation: why do strong interviewers bring it up for model selection?
options: It nests neural layers inside trees to create a single hybrid model
Outer loop estimates generalization; inner loop tunes hyperparameters without test leakage
It removes the need for any validation metric other than training loss
Nested CV is only required when using unsupervised clustering algorithms
questions.explanation: Nested CV separates selection from evaluation to reduce optimistic bias.

questions.stem: Domain shift: your val looks great but production fails. What do you investigate first?
options: Increase dropout until train accuracy drops below chance on every batch
Delete the test set so production metrics cannot disagree with val
Train/serving skew, population drift, label definition changes, and leakage artifacts
Switch metrics from F1 to silhouette score for all classification tasks
questions.explanation: Distribution and pipeline mismatches often explain validation–production gaps.

questions.stem: Cost-sensitive learning: how do you incorporate asymmetric FP/FN business costs?
options: Ignore costs and optimize accuracy only because costs are not ML concerns
Set the decision threshold to 0.5 always regardless of cost asymmetry
Convert the problem to unsupervised clustering to avoid false negatives
Weighted losses, tuned thresholds, or explicit cost matrices aligned to business
questions.explanation: Encode costs in loss weights and operating-point selection carefully.

questions.stem: Feature selection vs dimensionality reduction: how do you distinguish them cleanly?
options: Selection keeps original features; reduction projects to new derived axes (e.g., PCA)
They are identical operations with different library function names
Selection always creates new axes; PCA always keeps original column names
Reduction only works for labels; selection only works for unsupervised data
questions.explanation: Selection subsets features; reduction transforms the representation space.

questions.stem: When is a model with slightly worse offline metrics still the right production choice?
options: Never — offline leaderboard rank is the only production decision criterion
When it is cheaper, more robust to drift, explainable, or meets latency SLOs
Only when the worse model has strictly higher training loss by design
When legal teams forbid evaluating any online A/B experiment metrics
questions.explanation: Production constraints beyond a single metric often dominate the choice.

questions.stem: Label noise: what mitigation strategies should you mention at a senior interview level?
options: Fit harder until train accuracy is 100% so noise is memorized correctly
Remove the validation set because noisy labels only affect held-out data
Clean labels, robust losses, relabeling, confidence filtering, and noise-aware training
Noise is harmless for supervised learning and only hurts clustering
questions.explanation: Noisy targets need cleaning and/or algorithms robust to mislabels.

questions.stem: Why report confidence intervals or multiple-seed variance for ML results in interviews?
options: Intervals replace the need for a test set in modern ML workflows
Variance across seeds always means the model architecture is invalid
Interviewers only accept point estimates without any uncertainty talk
Single-run metrics can be noisy; uncertainty communicates estimate reliability
questions.explanation: Uncertainty-aware reporting shows maturity about experimental noise.

questions.stem: Semi-supervised learning: when is it a reasonable suggestion in an ML interview?
options: Abundant unlabeled data, scarce labels, and a structure that unlabeled data can help
When you have infinite labels and zero unlabeled examples by design
Only for reinforcement learning environments without any observations
Whenever accuracy is already perfect and you need more compute spend
questions.explanation: Semi-supervised leverages unlabeled structure when labels are expensive.

questions.stem: Metric gaming: why can optimizing a proxy metric hurt the real product objective?
options: Proxy metrics are mathematically identical to business KPIs always
Goodhart’s law — proxies can be gamed while true goals degrade
Product objectives cannot be influenced by model score thresholds
Only unsupervised metrics can be gamed; supervised metrics cannot
questions.explanation: Align evaluation with true outcomes and watch for proxy exploitation.
